item.title: Apple’s M4 Mac Mini is already the best value out there, and now it’s $99 off
item.itunes:title: Apple’s M4 Mac Mini is already the best value out there, and now it’s $99 off
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Apple Mac Mini (M4, 2024)

This is the latest desktop PC from Apple, delivering tons of power in a compact size. We loved this model in our review, and now it's even cheaper, thanks to this $100 discount.

$500 at Amazon

There are just so many things about a mini PC that are alluring. Not only do you get excellent power, but it also comes in a compact size that's perfect for any workspace. We've seen a lot of great models roll out over the past year, but Apple's Mac Mini really has to be at the top of the list, really excellent bang for your buck.

Related
Mac Mini (M4, 2024) review: I can't wait for Windows PCs to catch up

Apple is winning me over

14

With this deal, the price gets even better, with Amazon knocking $99 off the base model, which comes with an M4 chip and 16GB of RAM. This model can handle pretty much anything you throw at it, and we even scored it a 9/10 in our review. So, if you've been looking around for a mini PC with tons of power, now's going to be a great time to buy.

What's great about Apple's Mac Mini?

Since its release, Apple's M-series chips have really changed the tide when it comes to computer processors. Not only are M-series chips powerful, but they're also quite efficient, which means extended battery life in portable products like tablets and laptops.

When it comes to the Mac Mini, you're getting an M4 chip with 10 CPU and 10 GPU cores. As mentioned before, this particular model that's on sale comes with 16GB of RAM, and also features 256GB of internal storage. While this might not sound like a lot, it's actually plenty if you're planning on using it for everyday things like browsing the web, or performing some light photo or video editing.

Of course, you can also play games as well, but if you're looking to go that route, we recommend sticking with a gaming PC. Perhaps the only thing that we didn't really like about the Mac Mini was that there's no way to upgrade. So what you see is what you get, and what you'll be sticking with during the time you spend with it.

If that's a concern, you can always go with a higher-end model, but it will cost you a lot more. With that said, if you want to expand the Mac Mini a bit, we do recommend grabbing a docking station for it, since it's probably the best way to gain ports, while also making the power button easier to access.

Overall, you really can't go wrong here with the Mac Mini. It manages to deliver above expectations, and does so at a fantastic price. So, if you've been looking to go small and don't want to miss out on the power, then this Mac Mini is going to be right up your alley. Save $99 from Amazon while you can because this deal won't be around for long.

item.title: I stopped using Google Drive and OneDrive so I could host my own files — and I love it
item.itunes:title: I stopped using Google Drive and OneDrive so I could host my own files — and I love it
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Cloud storage is an incredibly useful technology that makes it easier than ever to keep copies of your files forever. But it also has a few downsides, including cost, the potential for someone to gain access to your account, or the simple fact that you're trusting a major corporation like Google or Microsoft to keep your data away from prying eyes. And when Microsoft has a list of file types that aren't allowed on OneDrive, you know the company has at least some access to that information.

Recently, I got into the rabbit hole of self-hosting, and Nextcloud and Immich have proven to the perfect solution for hosting my files, keeping them safe, private, and allowing me to share them with the people that matter.

Why I was done with cloud storage

Finding common ground was tough

The biggest reason I personally wanted to move away from cloud services was the cost. I was done with paying $7 per month for OneDrive a few months ago, but I was nearing the 40GB limit on my free account (I've used OneDrive for a long time so I have some loyalty bonuses).

Meanwhile, my wife has always used Google Drive and Photos, so we were also split across platforms and neither of us wanted to concede. I've never been a fan of Google services — not necessarily for any rational reason, but I refused to switch — and she had similar feelings about Microsoft.

Paying for two cloud services made no sense, and on top of that, these services cost a lot more than what we really wanted to spend. Sure, $10 per month for 2TB isn't a bad deal by any means, but we also wouldn't be using anywhere close to that maximum capacity. So we were in limbo for a long while until the idea of self-hosting came along.

 Related
Self-hosting vs. cloud storage: Which one really protects your data?

Between convenience and data control, both come with trade-offs

Nextcloud is perfect for file management

Everything is backed up and works as expected

The first service I wanted to self-host was Nextcloud, which is a fairly beat-for-beat replacement for OneDrive or Google Drive. The big difference is, of course, everything is stored on your own private server and you have total control over your data.

As a replacement for OneDrive and Google Drive, Nextcloud is everything I could want it to be and then some. At its core, it's a simple file storage and management tool, and it works perfectly as such. There isn't much that can go wrong here, but you can create folders, subfolders, and whatever you need to organize your files in the way you prefer, all while keeping them safe and sound on your own server. Nextcloud can also open PDF files out of the box, and it comes with a calendar, and even an email client, though I don't really use the latter.

Nextcloud allows you to create multiple users, too, so I could have my own account while my wife has one for herself, and our files are completely separate. But we can also share files with each other easily. It's even possible to share files with users outside our home network, though we haven't really touched this. I rarely ever share files with outsiders, and if I do, it's for work, where I need to use work resources anyway.

What's even better is that Nextcloud has a desktop app that can sync easily just like OneDrive. You just install Nextcloud, and your files become available in File Explorer, including the ability to download them to store locally on demand. This feature doesn't work on Mac, so I have to choose between downloading them all or not having them visible, but still, it works fairly well.

 Related
6 ways Nextcloud beats Microsoft 365 entirely for free

Nextcloud is the ultimate free Microsoft 365 alternative

9

I can even edit Office files

Nextcloud Office is a thing, too

One of the best things about Nextcloud is its extensibility. Nextcloud is an ecosystem in itself, with the ability to install apps such as Nextcloud Tasks, Passwords, and most importantly, Nextcloud Office. While I've already talked about Nextcloud Tasks before, Office is the most important part for me, and you can set it up in a couple of ways.

Nextcloud Office relies on Collabora Office, an Office suite based on LibreOffice that's made for online collaboration. To use Nextcloud Office, you need to set up a Collabora Office Document Editing (CODE) server, and you can either set up a separate instance on your NAS, or install the built-in CODE server in Nextcloud, which has slightly worse performance but makes things easier.

I went with the latter for simplicity, but it gets the job done just fine for the most part. I don't edit Office files very frequently, and most of the time, I'd rather work locally anyway, but it's nice to have this built-in feature that lets me open these Office files on any device to take a quick look or make a simple edit every now and then. I am considering setting up a standalone server, though, because I've noticed some performance hiccups when editing large Excel files.

 Related
This free self-hosted tool lets me sync my to-do lists across devices

Staying in sync while staying private

Immich is everything I need in a media storage service

Google Photos, but all mine

I could have a centralized service for storing files and photos, and Nextcloud does offer a useful app called Memories that offers a nice photo management experience. But I've heard a lot of good things about Immich, so I wanted to try setting this up instead, and I love it, too.

Everything about Immich is made to look and feel like Google Photos, bit this time, everything is stored on my own server and on my own terms. Photos and videos are automatically sorted in chronological order, and I can easily find memories from specific times in my life. I can also create albums for specific memories, such as trips and special events, all of which are also displayed chronologically.

What's more, despite running entirely locally, Immich also has AI features similar to Google Photos, so it can identify people in photos and make it easy to find pictures with specific objects or people.

Much like Nextcloud, Immich supports multiple user accounts, so my wife and I have separate storage, but I can share albums with her to include memories of our time together. And again, it's possible to share photos with users outside of our network, but this isn't something I've felt the need to do.

 Related
5 reasons Immich is better than Google Photos and iCloud

Compelling reasons to retain your privacy and self-host your photos and videos with Immich

4

Secure and remotely accessible

Reverse proxies and VPNs come in handy

Despite all of these files being stored on a server I have at home, I can still access all of them remotely when I'm outside. I've set up Tailscale to allow me to remotely connect to my home server using a tunneled connection, meaning the traffic sent between the server and my device can't be intercepted, and the connection is completely secure.

Even better, I also bought and set up a custom domain, so I can access these services even more easily with custom URLs set up in the Nginx Proxy Manager. This isn't mandatory, but it makes things almost as easy as a typical cloud service, so I don't have to miss the convenience of a typical cloud service.

That's not to say I don't use the cloud at all, though. I've set up a Backblaze account so that my TrueNAS server backs up my Nextcloud and Immich instances automatically on a daily basis. This gives me peace of mind while lowering the risk of exposing my data to strangers or companies that may use that data for who knows what. Plus, Backblaze is a lot cheaper than something like OneDrive or Google Drive.

 Related
Without even owning a domain, I enabled HTTPS to secure my self-hosted apps with Tailscale - here's how you can do it too

I grabbed free TLS certificates from Tailscale to enable HTTPS for the self-hosted apps in my home lab.

2

Nextcloud and Immich made self-hosting worth it

My self-hosting journey has led me to install a few different services I love, but Nextcloud and Immich alone have made the trouble of learning about self-hosting worth it. I love not having to depend on a cloud service where I'm not in full control, and I love that I can still access my files on any device, from anywhere.

It's only been a few weeks, but I already can't imagine going back to relying on typical cloud services for storing my files. I'm in control and I can change anything I want about how these files are stored and made accessible. It's a perfect solution.

  • Nextcloud
    See at Nextcloud
  • Immich
    See at Immich

My self-hosting journey has led me to install a few different services I love, but Nextcloud and Immich alone have made the trouble of learning about self-hosting worth it. I love not having to depend on a cloud service where I'm not in full control, and I love that I can still access my files on any device, from anywhere.

It's only been a few weeks, but I already can't imagine going back to relying on typical cloud services for storing my files. I'm in control and I can change anything I want about how these files are stored and made accessible. It's a perfect solution.

  • Nextcloud
    See at Nextcloud
  • Immich
    See at Immich

item.title: This free Steam Deck plugin adds better upscaling and frame generation to lots of games
item.itunes:title: This free Steam Deck plugin adds better upscaling and frame generation to lots of games
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The Steam Deck has long been a popular handheld gaming device thanks to its affordability and the SteamOS platform. Yet its hardware, while impressive for the price, can struggle with demanding AAA titles at smooth frame rates. A new free plugin for Decky Loader called FrameGen offers Steam Deck users a way to boost performance by using upscaling and frame generation for a growing list of games.

 Related
4 reasons I'm more excited about Valve's Steam Box than a Steam Deck 2

A modern Steam Box sounds more enticing than a handheld refresh, it could be Valve’s next big move.

43

How FrameGen works on the Steam Deck

FrameGen converts DLSS frame generation to FSR 3 frame generation

FrameGen works by replacing a game’s native NVIDIA DLSS frame generation module with AMD’s FSR. Unlike DLSS, which requires specific NVIDIA hardware, FSR is an open standard compatible with many devices, including the Steam Deck’s AMD-based hardware. This replacement means games that support DLSS frame generation but lack the FSR options can now benefit from FSR’s upscaling and frame generation, often doubling frame rates in some cases. For example, Plague Tale Requiem sees its rendering mode switch from DLSS super resolution to FSR 3 frame generation, resulting in much higher framerates and a smoother experience on the Steam Deck.

In addition to generating extra frames, FrameGen also taps into AMD’s FSR 3 upscaling technology to enhance image quality, even when games are rendered at lower resolutions for better performance. This results in both smoother animation and crisp visuals, avoiding the typical trade-offs like blurring or pixel artifacts seen with other upscaling methods. Steam Deck users can adjust the sharpness of the upscaling to fine-tune the experience, allowing them to prioritize either performance or clarity based on their preferences.

Benefits and performance improvements

Why FrameGen can breathe new life into demanding games

FrameGen’s primary advantage is the substantial increase in frame rates it brings to supported titles. Many demanding AAA games typically hover around 30 to 40 FPS on the Steam Deck, so doubling that output significantly improves responsiveness and gameplay feel. Titles like Hogwarts Legacy benefit from smoother motion through frame generation and added features such as adjustable upscale sharpness and the option for 2x frame generation. These enhancements contribute to better visual detail and smoother motion. The impact is even more pronounced on the Steam Deck OLED, where the 90Hz display allows players to fully appreciate the improved frame pacing and overall fluidity.

With FrameGen, the Steam Deck can now run a wider array of demanding games with better performance than before. This plugin extends the device’s lifespan by enabling it to compete with newer gaming handhelds through software rather than hardware upgrades. Players can enjoy improved frame rates in titles that previously struggled, such as Hogwarts Legacy or Final Fantasy XVI, making the experience more enjoyable on the go. FrameGen represents an exciting step forward for Steam Deck users who want to push their device beyond its default limits without waiting for next-generation hardware.

 Related
5 SteamOS 3 features that changed the way I game on Steam Deck

So, what are the best features of SteamOS 3? Let's see the 5 SteamOS 3 features that changed the way I game on Steam Deck.

Limitations and potential drawbacks

Frame generation trade-offs to consider

While FrameGen offers great performance gains, it is not without drawbacks. The mod only works with games running on DirectX 12 and can introduce increased input latency because the actual game still runs at the native frame rate while the mod generates extra frames. This may result in slightly less responsive controls during gameplay. Additionally, visual artifacts such as ghosting can possibly appear, especially in games with detailed foliage or fast-moving elements. These side effects vary by title and not all games are susceptible to them, but the mod can be disabled easily by turning off DLSS frame generation in game settings or uninstalling the FG mod via the FrameGen interface.

 Related
I can't use my Steam Deck anymore without these 9 plugins

The Steam Deck is already an incredible handheld gaming device, but with the right plugins, you can make it even better

4

A new lease on life for the Steam Deck’s game library

FrameGen is an innovative and free plugin that significantly improves the Steam Deck’s ability to run demanding games smoothly. Replacing NVIDIA’s DLSS frame generation with AMD’s FSR opens the door to higher frame rates and better upscaling on titles that previously had limited support. While it comes with some trade-offs in input latency and occasional visual artifacts, the extra performance makes it a valuable addition for Steam Deck owners. As community tools like FrameGen evolve, the Steam Deck continues to hold its place as a top handheld gaming device with extended longevity.

  • Your Rating

    Rate Now
    0/10

    Your comment has not been saved

    Follow
    Followed
    Steam Deck
    Dimensions
    11.7 x 4.6 x 1.9 inches (298 x 117 x 49mm)
    Brand
    Valve
    Weight
    1.48 pounds (669 grams)
    Chipset
    Custom AMD Zen 2-based processor (4 cores, 8 threads, up to 3.5GHz)
    RAM
    16GB LPDDR5 5600MHz
    Storage
    Up to 512GB M.2 2230 SSD

    Valve's first portable gaming PC, the Steam Deck, is one of the coolest handheld gaming consoles on the market, with a huge library of games to play and the ability to take them on the go.

    $399 at Steam
  • Steam Deck OLED
    Dimensions
    11.7 x 4.6 x 1.9 inches (298mm x 117mm x 49mm)
    Brand
    Valve
    Weight
    1.41 pounds (640 grams)
    Chipset
    Custom AMD Zen 2 APU (4 cores/8 threads, up to 3.5GHz boost)
    RAM
    16GB LPDDR5 6400MT/s
    Storage
    512GB or 1TB NVMe SSD, microSD card slot

    Valve's upgraded Steam Deck features a larger OLED display with HDR support, faster Wi-Fi, and a bigger battery. Plus, this new model is slightly lighter, has slightly faster RAM, and it comes with storage up to 1TB. If you're looking for the ultimate Steam Deck, this is the version for you.

    $649 at Steam

item.title: 6 tiny Windows utilities that solve annoying problems you didn't know you had
item.itunes:title: 6 tiny Windows utilities that solve annoying problems you didn't know you had
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When you use something all your life, it can be easy to overlook some of the problems it has. You grow so accustomed to things that they just work how you expect, even if it's not the most technically efficient. I suspect many people go through this with Windows 11, and to help address that, I've compiled a few utilities that can make your Windows experience that much better.

Whether it's disabling intrusive pop-ups or speeding up your searches, there are many small tools out there that can fix a lot of the gripes you may have with Windows 11, even if you don't realize you have them.

6 TidyOS

Remove bloatware and disable unwanted features

Microsoft's pushy ads and intrusive dialogs are one of the easier things to forget when you use your PC on a daily basis, but every now and then, they come back and reminds us of their existence. Whether it's the Start menu being chock-full of apps you don't need, the endless Microsoft 365 promotions, and the tips and tricks no one reads, there's a lot here to dislike, but you might not even think about it because you can usually dismiss or ignore these things.

 Related
If you want to debloat Windows, this free and open-source tool is one of the best out there

The magic of TidyOS is all in the name

11

But what if you didn't have to? TidyOS is a system debloating tool that can perform a wide range of tasks to make your Windows PC less of a nuisance. It can disable ads in the Start menu, the "finish setup" ads, uninstall preloaded apps, and disable all kinds of telemetry and other unnecessary features that might be eating into your resources. There's a lot to dive into here, but the UI for TidyOS is fairly simple, so you can get the hang of it quickly and make your Windows experience that much better.

TidyOS is just over 300KB to download, making it positively minuscule by today's standards, but it can really help clean up your PC.

TidyOS
See at Github

5 ExplorerPatcher

Fix the taskbar, Start menu, and more

I see it said occasionally that the Start menu on Windows 11 does the job just fine and there's no reason to replace it. And while I agree that it does work fine, you can always do better than that, and the Start menu isn't even the only thing that could be improved upon.

ExplorerPatcher is a tool that's only slightly bigger than 11MB to download, making it very easy to download, but it introduces a ton of improvements in that small size. For one thing, you can do things like remove the Recommended section of the Start menu, which Windows 11 normally doesn't let you do. That means you get much more space for your apps, and in tandem, you can access them more quickly.

 Related
I finally set up ExplorerPatcher on Windows, and I should have sooner

Windows 11 is much better this way

3

On top of that, ExplorerPatcher changes your taskbar so you can move it to any side of the screen, which is something you used to be able to do before Windows 11. If you didn't know that was possible, ExplorerPatcher gives you the perfect chance to try it out — you might just like it more than the default.

ExplorerPatcher
See at Github

4 TranslucentTB

Let's be clear here

One request I see semi-frequently online is the option to have a transparent taskbar that's a bit more reminiscent of something like macOS. Maybe you've never considered an option like this, but it's never too late to try it out for yourself, and it's possible thanks to TranslucentTB.

 Related
4 apps that give you the transparent taskbar Microsoft won't

You don't have to wait around for Microsoft to give you a transparent taskbar in Windows 11. Just check out these apps.

This app, which is just under 4MB in size on GitHub, does exactly what it says on the tin, and then some. With it, you can turn your Windows 11 taskbar completely transparent, or choose from a range of other styles. You can give it an accent color and choose the opacity level without applying a blur effect, or simply make the blur effect a bit softer and allow your desktop wallpaper to shine through a bit more.

Whatever your preference, this is a lightweight and free app, so you have nothing to lose by giving it a shot.

TranslucentTB
See at Microsoft Store

3 Everything

Find files faster

Searching your files on Windows isn't particularly fast. While you can generally find most of the important files on your PC, it can take a while for search results to fully load, and if you're searching for something more obscure, it's not guaranteed you can find it with the basic Windows file search.

 Related
8 reasons to stop using Windows Search and use Everything instead

Windows search can be a drag. For a better search experience, check out Everything from Voidtools.

1

Everything is a very small app, coming in at under 2MB, but it's incredible. In a matter of minutes (if not seconds), it indexes every single file on your PC, and once that's done, searching for absolutely anything you want becomes instantaneous. You can type in anything you want, and results load instantly, updating live for every key press. It's hard to imagine a search tool being any faster than this, and you can make it even more efficient by learning proper search syntax to find content in specific folders. Finding your files will never be the same with this app.

Everything
See at VoidTools See at PortableApps

2 Listary

Launch your apps quickly

Everything is a great app for finding files, but if you want to launch apps just as quickly, then Listary is the app for you. The latest version of Lsitary comes in at just over 12MB, and it's incredibly useful. It runs in the background and all you need to do is press Ctrl twice at any moment to open the launcher bar. Here, you can search for installed apps on your PC, as well as files, to find and launch them as quickly as possible.

Listary's search is much more responsive than using the Start menu or search bar built into Windows 11, so you'll launch your apps much more quickly. Plus, you can cusotmize actions like opening the containing folder for an app for file, making it easier to get things done.

 Related
6 reasons Listary is one of the best launchers on Windows

Listary is a powerful and fast search and launching utility. Here are some great reasons to use it.

Listary also has a file search aspect to it that's very similar to Everything, and in some ways it's even better. But what helps it stick out even more is that it makes it easy to upload files, too. When you open a file dialog, Listary shows you a quick link to the last folder you opened in File Explorer so you can get there much faster. And if that's not the right one, you can just type in the name of the folder you want to go to, so you still save a lot of time compared to browsing your files manually.

Listary
See at Listary

1 File Pilot

A file manager like no other

I tried to refrain from including paid apps here, but I couldn't ignore File Pilot when it fits this list so well. You may think that managing your files with File Explorer is fine. It is, in fact, totally serviceable most of the time, and it does work.

But when you try File Pilot for the first time, I'm confident you'll be blown away. The sheer responsiveness of opening a folder and moving back and forth through your files is a sight to behold. Everything responds and moves so swiftly in File Pilot, and managing your files becomes so much faster. You may not think much of it at first, but if you try to load a large folder with hundreds of files, it becomes very noticeable. I've had instances where File Explorer simply froze while trying to open one such folder, while File Pilot handled it gracefully.

 Related
5 reasons I use File Pilot to manage my files on Windows

File Pilot is a dream of a file manager

5

And that's not even mentioning the features like being able to instantly split a window to create a multi-pane view for more advanced file management, complete with tab support for each pane. Resizing thumbnails and changing view modes is instant, and everything just feels incredibly snappy.

The beta for File Pilot is free, but the final version will cost $50, which makes this harder to recommend. But if you try the beta, I can guarantee you'll be at least a little tempted to pay up. And if you're curious — the app is under 2MB to download, so you won't even notice it taking up any space on your PC.

File Pilot
See at File Pilot

Make a huge impact in all the right ways

Windows 11 may be just fine out of the box, but it's truly impressive how much more you can get out of your PC by just installing these very lightweight apps. They may not have a big impact on your storage or internet usage, but they'll make a huge impact on the experience, and I don't hesitate to recommend any of them. I've personally used and loved all of these, and you have nothing to lose by trying them out.

1

Everything is a very small app, coming in at under 2MB, but it's incredible. In a matter of minutes (if not seconds), it indexes every single file on your PC, and once that's done, searching for absolutely anything you want becomes instantaneous. You can type in anything you want, and results load instantly, updating live for every key press. It's hard to imagine a search tool being any faster than this, and you can make it even more efficient by learning proper search syntax to find content in specific folders. Finding your files will never be the same with this app.

Everything
See at VoidTools See at PortableApps

2 Listary

Launch your apps quickly

Everything is a great app for finding files, but if you want to launch apps just as quickly, then Listary is the app for you. The latest version of Lsitary comes in at just over 12MB, and it's incredibly useful. It runs in the background and all you need to do is press Ctrl twice at any moment to open the launcher bar. Here, you can search for installed apps on your PC, as well as files, to find and launch them as quickly as possible.

Listary's search is much more responsive than using the Start menu or search bar built into Windows 11, so you'll launch your apps much more quickly. Plus, you can cusotmize actions like opening the containing folder for an app for file, making it easier to get things done.

 Related
6 reasons Listary is one of the best launchers on Windows

Listary is a powerful and fast search and launching utility. Here are some great reasons to use it.

Listary also has a file search aspect to it that's very similar to Everything, and in some ways it's even better. But what helps it stick out even more is that it makes it easy to upload files, too. When you open a file dialog, Listary shows you a quick link to the last folder you opened in File Explorer so you can get there much faster. And if that's not the right one, you can just type in the name of the folder you want to go to, so you still save a lot of time compared to browsing your files manually.

Listary
See at Listary

1 File Pilot

A file manager like no other

I tried to refrain from including paid apps here, but I couldn't ignore File Pilot when it fits this list so well. You may think that managing your files with File Explorer is fine. It is, in fact, totally serviceable most of the time, and it does work.

But when you try File Pilot for the first time, I'm confident you'll be blown away. The sheer responsiveness of opening a folder and moving back and forth through your files is a sight to behold. Everything responds and moves so swiftly in File Pilot, and managing your files becomes so much faster. You may not think much of it at first, but if you try to load a large folder with hundreds of files, it becomes very noticeable. I've had instances where File Explorer simply froze while trying to open one such folder, while File Pilot handled it gracefully.

 Related
5 reasons I use File Pilot to manage my files on Windows

File Pilot is a dream of a file manager

5

And that's not even mentioning the features like being able to instantly split a window to create a multi-pane view for more advanced file management, complete with tab support for each pane. Resizing thumbnails and changing view modes is instant, and everything just feels incredibly snappy.

The beta for File Pilot is free, but the final version will cost $50, which makes this harder to recommend. But if you try the beta, I can guarantee you'll be at least a little tempted to pay up. And if you're curious — the app is under 2MB to download, so you won't even notice it taking up any space on your PC.

File Pilot
See at File Pilot

Make a huge impact in all the right ways

Windows 11 may be just fine out of the box, but it's truly impressive how much more you can get out of your PC by just installing these very lightweight apps. They may not have a big impact on your storage or internet usage, but they'll make a huge impact on the experience, and I don't hesitate to recommend any of them. I've personally used and loved all of these, and you have nothing to lose by trying them out.
item.content:encoded:
When you use something all your life, it can be easy to overlook some of the problems it has. You grow so accustomed to things that they just work how you expect, even if it's not the most technically efficient. I suspect many people go through this with Windows 11, and to help address that, I've compiled a few utilities that can make your Windows experience that much better.

Whether it's disabling intrusive pop-ups or speeding up your searches, there are many small tools out there that can fix a lot of the gripes you may have with Windows 11, even if you don't realize you have them.

6 TidyOS

Remove bloatware and disable unwanted features

Microsoft's pushy ads and intrusive dialogs are one of the easier things to forget when you use your PC on a daily basis, but every now and then, they come back and reminds us of their existence. Whether it's the Start menu being chock-full of apps you don't need, the endless Microsoft 365 promotions, and the tips and tricks no one reads, there's a lot here to dislike, but you might not even think about it because you can usually dismiss or ignore these things.

 Related
If you want to debloat Windows, this free and open-source tool is one of the best out there

The magic of TidyOS is all in the name

11

But what if you didn't have to? TidyOS is a system debloating tool that can perform a wide range of tasks to make your Windows PC less of a nuisance. It can disable ads in the Start menu, the "finish setup" ads, uninstall preloaded apps, and disable all kinds of telemetry and other unnecessary features that might be eating into your resources. There's a lot to dive into here, but the UI for TidyOS is fairly simple, so you can get the hang of it quickly and make your Windows experience that much better.

TidyOS is just over 300KB to download, making it positively minuscule by today's standards, but it can really help clean up your PC.

TidyOS
See at Github

5 ExplorerPatcher

Fix the taskbar, Start menu, and more

I see it said occasionally that the Start menu on Windows 11 does the job just fine and there's no reason to replace it. And while I agree that it does work fine, you can always do better than that, and the Start menu isn't even the only thing that could be improved upon.

ExplorerPatcher is a tool that's only slightly bigger than 11MB to download, making it very easy to download, but it introduces a ton of improvements in that small size. For one thing, you can do things like remove the Recommended section of the Start menu, which Windows 11 normally doesn't let you do. That means you get much more space for your apps, and in tandem, you can access them more quickly.

 Related
I finally set up ExplorerPatcher on Windows, and I should have sooner

Windows 11 is much better this way

3

On top of that, ExplorerPatcher changes your taskbar so you can move it to any side of the screen, which is something you used to be able to do before Windows 11. If you didn't know that was possible, ExplorerPatcher gives you the perfect chance to try it out — you might just like it more than the default.

ExplorerPatcher
See at Github

4 TranslucentTB

Let's be clear here

One request I see semi-frequently online is the option to have a transparent taskbar that's a bit more reminiscent of something like macOS. Maybe you've never considered an option like this, but it's never too late to try it out for yourself, and it's possible thanks to TranslucentTB.

 Related
4 apps that give you the transparent taskbar Microsoft won't

You don't have to wait around for Microsoft to give you a transparent taskbar in Windows 11. Just check out these apps.

This app, which is just under 4MB in size on GitHub, does exactly what it says on the tin, and then some. With it, you can turn your Windows 11 taskbar completely transparent, or choose from a range of other styles. You can give it an accent color and choose the opacity level without applying a blur effect, or simply make the blur effect a bit softer and allow your desktop wallpaper to shine through a bit more.

Whatever your preference, this is a lightweight and free app, so you have nothing to lose by giving it a shot.

TranslucentTB
See at Microsoft Store

3 Everything

Find files faster

Searching your files on Windows isn't particularly fast. While you can generally find most of the important files on your PC, it can take a while for search results to fully load, and if you're searching for something more obscure, it's not guaranteed you can find it with the basic Windows file search.

 Related
8 reasons to stop using Windows Search and use Everything instead

Windows search can be a drag. For a better search experience, check out Everything from Voidtools.

1

Everything is a very small app, coming in at under 2MB, but it's incredible. In a matter of minutes (if not seconds), it indexes every single file on your PC, and once that's done, searching for absolutely anything you want becomes instantaneous. You can type in anything you want, and results load instantly, updating live for every key press. It's hard to imagine a search tool being any faster than this, and you can make it even more efficient by learning proper search syntax to find content in specific folders. Finding your files will never be the same with this app.

Everything
See at VoidTools See at PortableApps

2 Listary

Launch your apps quickly

Everything is a great app for finding files, but if you want to launch apps just as quickly, then Listary is the app for you. The latest version of Lsitary comes in at just over 12MB, and it's incredibly useful. It runs in the background and all you need to do is press Ctrl twice at any moment to open the launcher bar. Here, you can search for installed apps on your PC, as well as files, to find and launch them as quickly as possible.

Listary's search is much more responsive than using the Start menu or search bar built into Windows 11, so you'll launch your apps much more quickly. Plus, you can cusotmize actions like opening the containing folder for an app for file, making it easier to get things done.

 Related
6 reasons Listary is one of the best launchers on Windows

Listary is a powerful and fast search and launching utility. Here are some great reasons to use it.

Listary also has a file search aspect to it that's very similar to Everything, and in some ways it's even better. But what helps it stick out even more is that it makes it easy to upload files, too. When you open a file dialog, Listary shows you a quick link to the last folder you opened in File Explorer so you can get there much faster. And if that's not the right one, you can just type in the name of the folder you want to go to, so you still save a lot of time compared to browsing your files manually.

Listary
See at Listary

1 File Pilot

A file manager like no other

I tried to refrain from including paid apps here, but I couldn't ignore File Pilot when it fits this list so well. You may think that managing your files with File Explorer is fine. It is, in fact, totally serviceable most of the time, and it does work.

But when you try File Pilot for the first time, I'm confident you'll be blown away. The sheer responsiveness of opening a folder and moving back and forth through your files is a sight to behold. Everything responds and moves so swiftly in File Pilot, and managing your files becomes so much faster. You may not think much of it at first, but if you try to load a large folder with hundreds of files, it becomes very noticeable. I've had instances where File Explorer simply froze while trying to open one such folder, while File Pilot handled it gracefully.

 Related
5 reasons I use File Pilot to manage my files on Windows

File Pilot is a dream of a file manager

5

And that's not even mentioning the features like being able to instantly split a window to create a multi-pane view for more advanced file management, complete with tab support for each pane. Resizing thumbnails and changing view modes is instant, and everything just feels incredibly snappy.

The beta for File Pilot is free, but the final version will cost $50, which makes this harder to recommend. But if you try the beta, I can guarantee you'll be at least a little tempted to pay up. And if you're curious — the app is under 2MB to download, so you won't even notice it taking up any space on your PC.

File Pilot
See at File Pilot

Make a huge impact in all the right ways

Windows 11 may be just fine out of the box, but it's truly impressive how much more you can get out of your PC by just installing these very lightweight apps. They may not have a big impact on your storage or internet usage, but they'll make a huge impact on the experience, and I don't hesitate to recommend any of them. I've personally used and loved all of these, and you have nothing to lose by trying them out.

item.title: Setting up Notion on Windows is getting easier as it arrives on the Microsoft Store
item.itunes:title: Setting up Notion on Windows is getting easier as it arrives on the Microsoft Store
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Notion is now available on the Microsoft Store for easier access on Windows devices.
  • Notion allows users to organize their lives with notes, project management, and more.
  • Despite its benefits, Notion has some drawbacks according to some users.

If you use Notion to manage your notes, you'll likely want the app on every device you own for maximum productivity. And while Notion isn't on every app and operating system that people want it to be on, it's making its way around.

Now, Notion has announced that it has finally gotten around to adding the app to the Microsoft Store. So, if you're setting up a new PC and want to get your notes up and running ASAP, you can do so via the Store.

 Related
This free self-hosted alternative to Notion is the best PKM tool out there

It has most of the Notion features

1

Notion arrives on the Microsoft Store

In a post on X, Notion made an announcement that the app has appeared on the Microsoft Store. Given how the Store appears as a pre-installed app on Windows machines, this means that people grabbing a new PC don't even need to fire up a browser to get access to their Notion notes.

If you want to install the app, head over to the Notion Microsoft Store page and give it a download.

If this is the first time you've heard of Notion, it's an app that lets you organise your life down to the details. It lets you take notes, organise your day, manage projects, and keep track of your outgoings really easily. One of our writers, Parth Shah, wrote a guide on using Notion to stay on top of his life, making tools such as a subscription tracker and a reading list in the app.

We also have a few more pieces on the benefits of Notion:

  • 5 reasons Notion is my ultimate writing tool over everything else
  • Is Notion still the best productivity app?
  • 6 lessons learned building a Notion system for a multimillion-dollar advertising company

However, while Notion may be a lifesaver for some, it's by no means a perfect app. Here are some more pieces to balance out our thoughts of the app:

  • 5 reasons why I use anything but Notion as a second brain
  • Notion is a jack of all trades and master of none
  • Notion is really starting to fall behind alternatives, and I can't see myself sticking around

item.title: Why you shouldn't install NVMe SSD caching inside your Windows PC
item.itunes:title: Why you shouldn't install NVMe SSD caching inside your Windows PC
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The solid-state drive (SSD) has transformed the way we store data, paving the way to the modern smartphone, super-fast PC storage, and the thinnest laptops money can buy. They also allowed us to improve the performance of older or slower hardware, namely, mechanical hard drives. Intel Optane, AMD StoreMI, and some other technologies made their way to the datacenter, but you can take advantage of them at home with a server, NAS, or even your Windows PC. The thing is, only the first two scenarios are worthy of NVMe caching as your desktop PC likely already has fast enough storage and memory to handle everything you throw at it.

Here are a few reasons why NVMe caching on your Windows Pc is likely more trouble than it's worth.

5 You likely won't benefit from caching

Do you even edit video, bro?

If you only use your desktop PC to play games, browse some websites, watch some videos, and occasionally transfer files here and there, you won't notice much of a benefit from NVMe SSD caching. Then there's the fact you're probably already using an NVMe SSD for storing your OS and/or data. This causes an issue since NVMe caching won't magically improve the speed of an NVMe storage pool since they're going to run at about the same speeds, so it makes more sense for the system to continue using the primary NVMe drive. A modern OS is smart enough to use the faster NVMe drive for virtual memory files and any other caching that needs to occur outside of RAM.

Your RAM is always going to be faster, which is why we always recommend upgrading your system memory to faster and higher-capacity modules to get the most out of your PC. NVMe caching would not have much of an impact on general performance, especially since your PC isn't typically used to store and access data, but rather carry out various tasks that can better take advantage of more powerful hardware.

 Related
I set up an NVMe cache in my PC, and I learned that most people don't need one

Cache me outside, how bout dat?

2

4 Your Windows PC's NVMe SSD is already fast enough

Storage is much faster these days

That leads us to the fact that your NVMe SSD is likely already fast enough inside your PC. If it's a PCIe 4.0 or PCIe 5.0 SSD, you're pushing speeds of 7,000 MB/s or more, that's 7 GB/s. You could transfer 2,000 .mp3 files at 3 MB a piece in a second. That's how fast these modern SSDs are. So much so that we often recommend against spending more on a PCIe 5.0 drive unless you can fully utilize the additional bandwidth (and your PC fully supports it). Do you need to improve something already running far beyond what you likely require? Probably not, which makes SSD caching moot on a PC.

If you have an old PC with mechanical drives, NVMe caching could make sense, but then I'd suggest simply upgrading your drive(s) to faster SSDs anyway.

 Related
7 SSD marketing terms that don’t mean what you think they do

It's worth digging deeper when bombarded with SSD marketing

3 Other parts would provide more gains

GPU, RAM, and CPU

Instead of thinking about NVMe SSD caching, why not consider upgrading your GPU, CPU, or RAM? This can prove challenging with a laptop since most have these parts soldered to the main motherboard, but desktop PCs usually have standardized parts that can be swapped out. NVMe caching won't make much difference to a gaming PC with a primary solid-state drive, but a faster graphics card with more VRAM most certainly would. The same goes for your CPU if you use the system for virtualization or work with heavier software.

Remember that NVMe SSD caching essentially acts like a type of RAM for frequently accessed files. Adding more RAM to your PC will improve overall performance since the OS can make better use of more capacity, as well as faster transfer speeds should you increase the speed (DDR5-6000 to DDR5-7200) and your motherboard supports it.

 Related
I never upgrade my PC if it can still do these 5 things

Hit the brakes on that upgrade if your PC can still do this

10

2 Install cache on your NAS or home server

Have more of an impact

Running network-attached storage (NAS) or home servers is a great way to keep your data safe, save some money on cloud subscription services, and create your own apps. It's easy to go from zero to hero with your own password manager, recipe manager, media streaming platform, cloud storage solution, Microsoft 365 alternative, and more. You're likely using a few slower SATA SSDs or HDDs, which top out at around 500 MB/s. That's still ample for loading most data, but this can cause issues when working with seriously large files or lots of data. NVMe SSD caching can help with this by acting as slower RAM, where frequently accessed files can be stored for quicker access.

You'll see much more of a positive impact by using NVMe caching with a NAS or home server, especially when using mechanical drives, and particularly those with slower motors. Depending on what your current workload is with the NAS or home server, you could expect to see more throughput and quicker load times with various applications. A modern desktop or laptop PC generally has an NVMe SSD for primary storage, so it can already utilize its performance to unlock better load times on software and quicker rates for file transfers. A server or NAS isn't so lucky since NVMe storage is incredibly expensive and doesn't provide many of the benefits NAS drives can, namely, capacity.

 Related
7 reasons SSD caching is the smartest upgrade for my home server

A small NVMe can make a big difference

1 It's expensive (for what you get)

Money better spent elsewhere

I've already alluded to the cost of modern NVMe storage, especially for storing data, but it rings true for caching too. You'll need to fork out a considerable sum of money for an NVMe drive. While a budget-friendly SSD could cut it, it won't provide much of a benefit since it's likely slow and has weaker endurance. Intel Optane drives are incredibly expensive in comparison. It's funds that could be better spent elsewhere on your system, as aforementioned, with potential GPU, CPU, and RAM upgrades.

 Related
How you can turn an old SSD into a high-performance cache for your NAS

Upgrade your RAM with some speedy SSD caching.

6

Get more from your caching elsewhere

Building out a home lab is a great project for learning new things, saving some money in the long run, and creating some truly wacky and wonderful installations. Whether you're combining a few prebuilt NAS enclosures or plan to go all out with second-hand enterprise hardware, SSD caching can prove useful when attempting to get more from slower hardware, be it RAM or drives. It can have more of an impact with older hardware, especially those without support for more recent RAM generations or slots for speedier storage SSDs. Just make sure you have an available NVMe M.2 slot and your CPU has ample PCI lanes to handle the bandwidth.

For desktop PCs, try to see if upgrading the CPU, RAM, or GPU will make a difference to your daily workloads.

Whether you're combining a few prebuilt NAS enclosures or plan to go all out with second-hand enterprise hardware, SSD caching can prove useful when attempting to get more from slower hardware, be it RAM or drives. It can have more of an impact with older hardware, especially those without support for more recent RAM generations or slots for speedier storage SSDs. Just make sure you have an available NVMe M.2 slot and your CPU has ample PCI lanes to handle the bandwidth.

For desktop PCs, try to see if upgrading the CPU, RAM, or GPU will make a difference to your daily workloads.
item.content:encoded:
The solid-state drive (SSD) has transformed the way we store data, paving the way to the modern smartphone, super-fast PC storage, and the thinnest laptops money can buy. They also allowed us to improve the performance of older or slower hardware, namely, mechanical hard drives. Intel Optane, AMD StoreMI, and some other technologies made their way to the datacenter, but you can take advantage of them at home with a server, NAS, or even your Windows PC. The thing is, only the first two scenarios are worthy of NVMe caching as your desktop PC likely already has fast enough storage and memory to handle everything you throw at it.

Here are a few reasons why NVMe caching on your Windows Pc is likely more trouble than it's worth.

5 You likely won't benefit from caching

Do you even edit video, bro?

If you only use your desktop PC to play games, browse some websites, watch some videos, and occasionally transfer files here and there, you won't notice much of a benefit from NVMe SSD caching. Then there's the fact you're probably already using an NVMe SSD for storing your OS and/or data. This causes an issue since NVMe caching won't magically improve the speed of an NVMe storage pool since they're going to run at about the same speeds, so it makes more sense for the system to continue using the primary NVMe drive. A modern OS is smart enough to use the faster NVMe drive for virtual memory files and any other caching that needs to occur outside of RAM.

Your RAM is always going to be faster, which is why we always recommend upgrading your system memory to faster and higher-capacity modules to get the most out of your PC. NVMe caching would not have much of an impact on general performance, especially since your PC isn't typically used to store and access data, but rather carry out various tasks that can better take advantage of more powerful hardware.

 Related
I set up an NVMe cache in my PC, and I learned that most people don't need one

Cache me outside, how bout dat?

2

4 Your Windows PC's NVMe SSD is already fast enough

Storage is much faster these days

That leads us to the fact that your NVMe SSD is likely already fast enough inside your PC. If it's a PCIe 4.0 or PCIe 5.0 SSD, you're pushing speeds of 7,000 MB/s or more, that's 7 GB/s. You could transfer 2,000 .mp3 files at 3 MB a piece in a second. That's how fast these modern SSDs are. So much so that we often recommend against spending more on a PCIe 5.0 drive unless you can fully utilize the additional bandwidth (and your PC fully supports it). Do you need to improve something already running far beyond what you likely require? Probably not, which makes SSD caching moot on a PC.

If you have an old PC with mechanical drives, NVMe caching could make sense, but then I'd suggest simply upgrading your drive(s) to faster SSDs anyway.

 Related
7 SSD marketing terms that don’t mean what you think they do

It's worth digging deeper when bombarded with SSD marketing

3 Other parts would provide more gains

GPU, RAM, and CPU

Instead of thinking about NVMe SSD caching, why not consider upgrading your GPU, CPU, or RAM? This can prove challenging with a laptop since most have these parts soldered to the main motherboard, but desktop PCs usually have standardized parts that can be swapped out. NVMe caching won't make much difference to a gaming PC with a primary solid-state drive, but a faster graphics card with more VRAM most certainly would. The same goes for your CPU if you use the system for virtualization or work with heavier software.

Remember that NVMe SSD caching essentially acts like a type of RAM for frequently accessed files. Adding more RAM to your PC will improve overall performance since the OS can make better use of more capacity, as well as faster transfer speeds should you increase the speed (DDR5-6000 to DDR5-7200) and your motherboard supports it.

 Related
I never upgrade my PC if it can still do these 5 things

Hit the brakes on that upgrade if your PC can still do this

10

2 Install cache on your NAS or home server

Have more of an impact

Running network-attached storage (NAS) or home servers is a great way to keep your data safe, save some money on cloud subscription services, and create your own apps. It's easy to go from zero to hero with your own password manager, recipe manager, media streaming platform, cloud storage solution, Microsoft 365 alternative, and more. You're likely using a few slower SATA SSDs or HDDs, which top out at around 500 MB/s. That's still ample for loading most data, but this can cause issues when working with seriously large files or lots of data. NVMe SSD caching can help with this by acting as slower RAM, where frequently accessed files can be stored for quicker access.

You'll see much more of a positive impact by using NVMe caching with a NAS or home server, especially when using mechanical drives, and particularly those with slower motors. Depending on what your current workload is with the NAS or home server, you could expect to see more throughput and quicker load times with various applications. A modern desktop or laptop PC generally has an NVMe SSD for primary storage, so it can already utilize its performance to unlock better load times on software and quicker rates for file transfers. A server or NAS isn't so lucky since NVMe storage is incredibly expensive and doesn't provide many of the benefits NAS drives can, namely, capacity.

 Related
7 reasons SSD caching is the smartest upgrade for my home server

A small NVMe can make a big difference

1 It's expensive (for what you get)

Money better spent elsewhere

I've already alluded to the cost of modern NVMe storage, especially for storing data, but it rings true for caching too. You'll need to fork out a considerable sum of money for an NVMe drive. While a budget-friendly SSD could cut it, it won't provide much of a benefit since it's likely slow and has weaker endurance. Intel Optane drives are incredibly expensive in comparison. It's funds that could be better spent elsewhere on your system, as aforementioned, with potential GPU, CPU, and RAM upgrades.

 Related
How you can turn an old SSD into a high-performance cache for your NAS

Upgrade your RAM with some speedy SSD caching.

6

Get more from your caching elsewhere

Building out a home lab is a great project for learning new things, saving some money in the long run, and creating some truly wacky and wonderful installations. Whether you're combining a few prebuilt NAS enclosures or plan to go all out with second-hand enterprise hardware, SSD caching can prove useful when attempting to get more from slower hardware, be it RAM or drives. It can have more of an impact with older hardware, especially those without support for more recent RAM generations or slots for speedier storage SSDs. Just make sure you have an available NVMe M.2 slot and your CPU has ample PCI lanes to handle the bandwidth.

For desktop PCs, try to see if upgrading the CPU, RAM, or GPU will make a difference to your daily workloads.

item.title: These 5 tools keep my Google Drive organized
item.itunes:title: These 5 tools keep my Google Drive organized
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I started using Google Drive as a convenient and free cloud storage solution, but it quickly became a digital junkyard. Files were everywhere, important documents were hard to track down, and collaboration always felt chaotic due to disorganization. It was nearly impossible to find the specific files when I needed them the most. Finding files felt like a treasure hunt. After struggling with this disarray, I discovered these five tools that transformed my Google Drive from a cluttered mess into an organized system.

 Related
How to set up and use Google Drive on Windows

Unlock the power of Google Drive on your Windows computer

1

5 Save to Google Drive

Ultimate time-saver for researchers out there

Extensive internet research is a core requirement of my work. To ensure I can revisit material anytime, anywhere, I habitually store my findings on Google Drive. Before I came across this tiny tool called Save to Google Drive, this process was quite inefficient and tedious, as it involved downloading everything locally before uploading it again to my Drive.

With the Save to Google Drive extension installed, I can now transfer content directly from the internet into my Google Drive. As a blogger, I need to save research for different topics into separate folders for easy accessibility. Save to Drive lets me save the information to the desired location on the drive. It lets me save information directly to the desired location within my Drive, including images, videos, documents, and URLs. This tool has significantly streamlined my research workflow, saving considerable time and effort.

Save to Google Drive
See at Google

4 Filerev

Everyone loves a clean and smooth drive

Filerev is primarily a cloud storage management and cleanup tool designed to help users analyze, organize, and free up space in their Google Drive accounts. I frequently save large videos and documents to Google Drive, but forget to delete them. Filerev helps me identify and manage these large, forgotten files. A single scan provides a comprehensive analysis of all files within the storage. It helps find and clean up hidden files, large files, duplicate files, very old files, and empty folders.

Filerev's free plan allows for one scan per month. It scans up to 1 million files and folders and includes up to 500 file and folder move or deletion operations per month. You can also go for the premium plans starting from $48 per year, which include significant features like multiple scans, bulk deletion, folder-based scanning, and many other benefits.

Filerev
See at Filerev

3 Drive Explorer

No need to drive within the drive!

While Google Drive provides smooth storage and accessibility, its standard interface lacks a centralized view for managing all your content and metadata comprehensively. Drive Explorer solves this by allowing you to export file and folder metadata from your Google Drive, including My Drive, Shared Drives, and files shared with you, directly to a Google Sheet. It creates a single list featuring essential details like file names, paths, sizes, share statuses, various links (including direct downloads and image URLs), and almost every metadata present with the content. I can literally transform my cloud storage structure into an organized, accessible dataset within a spreadsheet.

I primarily utilize Drive Explorer to easily access and generate download links, and it’s a real time saver. The best part is that after exporting once, the automatic refresh option takes care of everything. It keeps the file list up-to-date. This saves considerable time and boosts productivity for heavy Google Drive users like me.

Drive Explorer
See at Google

2 Zip Extractor

Manage archived files like a pro

I maintain multiple Google accounts, and for all but one, I manage the storage within the 15GB of free space provided by Google. For that, I heavily rely on ZIP and RAR files, especially for storing large items such as software installers, media files, and chat backups. I convert large software and media files to ZIP files and store them on Google Drive. However, managing these compressed archive files directly within Google Drive previously proved to be a significant challenge. A common problem was that folders containing archive contents often appeared empty when viewed through the standard Drive interface. Fortunately, after installing the Zip Extractor, these issues have been resolved.

I just connected Zip Extractor with my Google Drive account once, and every time I can access compressed files directly from the drive. Once I open them with Zip Extractor, the contents are displayed instantly in a browser tab. I can view them or extract them at my desired location within the drive as well.

Beyond its extraction capabilities for RAR and ZIP formats, ZIP Extractor also functions as a file compression tool. Users can select multiple files and easily compress them into a new ZIP archive, which can then be saved directly back into Google Drive.

Always extract content in a temporary folder within Drive, which simplifies cleanup after viewing or editing and helps keep your main folders organized.

ZIP Extractor
See at ZIP Extractor
Related
4 reasons you should stop using WinRAR and switch to 7-Zip

7-Zip has multiple benefits over WinRAR

9

1 Cloud Drive to Mail

Finding it hard getting attached? No worries!

I store important documents, including personal files and my resume, on Google Drive for convenient access. However, emailing these documents requires either sharing a link or downloading the file locally for attachment to Gmail. Unfortunately, there’s no direct way I can attach the document from the drive to my email. This tiny tool called Cloud Drive to Mail bridges the gap for me, and lets me send documents as email attachments directly from their Google Drive. It allows attachment of up to 22 to 25 megabytes in size.

It can be easily installed within Google Drive, and once it is installed it enables users to open any file from the drive with its mail editor. This old-fashioned editor features a straightforward interface, allowing users to add attachments, compose a subject and body content, include CC recipients, and even send a copy to themselves.

Cloud Drive to Mail
See at Google
Related
5 reasons Gmail isn’t the best email client anymore

The Gmail web interface might work well for some, but it certainly isn't the best email client you can use.

5

Organized Google Drive is a real productivity booster!

If Google Drive is your digital hub where you store projects, collaborate on documents, and manage your daily workflow, then its organization is key. Misplaced files and chaotic folders drain your time and focus; a well-organized Google Drive directly boosts daily productivity. Fortunately, these productive Google Drive tools combat disorganization and keep your Drive running smoothly. They help you find documents quickly, seamlessly attach files to emails, clear out unnecessary junk, and ultimately free your mind to focus on your tasks.

ZIP Extractor
See at ZIP Extractor
Related

7-Zip has multiple benefits over WinRAR

9

It can be easily installed within Google Drive, and once it is installed it enables users to open any file from the drive with its mail editor. This old-fashioned editor features a straightforward interface, allowing users to add attachments, compose a subject and body content, include CC recipients, and even send a copy to themselves.

Cloud Drive to Mail
See at Google
Related

The Gmail web interface might work well for some, but it certainly isn't the best email client you can use.

5
